figures.caption: Figure 8: Ablation results. Red boxes highlight artifacts from omitting structure lines (jagged boundaries and detail loss) or cross-fields (irregular edge flow and topological corruption).
Figure 7: Comparison with FlowRep. From left: Input, FlowRep, and TopGen. We visualize predicted structure lines and final quad-meshes with timing reported below.
Figure 2: A sample from TopGen-220K dataset. From left to right: the input triangular mesh, the structural layout, the cross-field, and the quadrilateral mesh.

Figure 9: Qualitative comparison with NeurCross. (Top to bottom) AI-generated models (Robot, Monster) and QuadWild-300 samples (Mech09, Rod). While NeurCross exhibits numerical instability on AI-generated inputs, our method consistently achieves superior results, ensuring both precise external geometric profiles and smooth internal edge flows across all sources.

Figure 6: Qualitative comparison with quadrilateral remeshing methods. (a) Input meshes: AI-generated (Robot, Monster) and QuadWild-300 samples (Mech09, Rod). (e) Results from InstantMeshes, QuadriFlow, ,QuadWild and Our TopGen.

Figure 4: Overview of the TopGen pipeline. Our framework first samples point clouds from the input mesh via the SES strategy [4]. A geometry-aware encoder then maps these points into a latent space. Subsequently, our proposed Dual-Query Decoder concurrently decodes the structural layouts and cross-fields in parallel. Finally, both predictions serve as joint guidance to facilitate high-quality quadrilateral remeshing.
